symptoms.symptom_name: Sudden crop of small red teardrop/drop-shaped papules on the trunk

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: The guttate ('drop-like') morphology on the trunk is the defining feature.
A drug eruption is usually confluent morbilliform macules rather than discrete scaly drop-shaped papules.
The post-streptococcal temporal trigger is the classic precipitant of guttate psoriasis and reframes the rash away from the antibiotic itself.

symptoms.symptom_name: Fine silvery scale on the papules

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Fine silvery/micaceous scale is characteristic of psoriasis and distinguishes it from a drug eruption or viral exanthem.
Meningococcal purpura is non-blanching; a fully blanching rash argues strongly against it.
Erythematous psoriatic papules blanch under pressure; consistent with a benign inflammatory eruption.
Absence of fever with a well appearance makes invasive meningococcal disease very unlikely.

symptoms.symptom_name: No lip/tongue swelling, no dyspnoea, no wheeze, no urticarial wheals

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Anaphylactic / urticarial drug reaction to penicillin
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of angioedema, airway compromise, urticaria and circulatory features excludes anaphylaxis.
Mild itch is common in guttate psoriasis but non-specific across many rashes.

symptoms.symptom_name: No herald patch preceding the eruption

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of a preceding herald patch makes pityriasis rosea less likely and favours guttate psoriasis given the post-strep timeline.

symptoms.symptom_name: Recently completed oral penicillin course (the anchoring red herring)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Recent penicillin exposure is the plausible red herring for a drug eruption, but morphology and the post-strep trigger point elsewhere.

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Anaphylactic / urticarial drug reaction to penicillin
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Prior exposure is necessary for sensitisation but is not itself evidence of an allergic reaction in the absence of allergic features.
Corroborates the recent streptococcal infection that triggered the eruption; a clinician palpation finding not available via telehealth.

symptoms.symptom_name: Normal vital signs (BP 120/75, HR 82, RR 16, SpO2 100%, temp 37.1)

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Anaphylactic / urticarial drug reaction to penicillin
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Normal haemodynamics and oxygenation exclude the circulatory and respiratory compromise of anaphylaxis; these are clinician-measured and sealed from the AI Doctor.
Normal observations and afebrile state are reassuring against invasive sepsis.

symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: Sudden scaly blanching drop-shaped truncal papules 1-2 weeks after strep pharyngitis is the diagnostic constellation.
Blanching rash, afebrile, no airway/urticarial features and normal observations together exclude the two time-critical mimics.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Glass (tumbler) test for blanching — patient-performed at point of contact
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: The single most useful bedside discriminator against meningococcal purpura; the AI Doctor can coach the patient to perform it live.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Throat swab / ASO or anti-DNase B titre to confirm recent streptococcal infection
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Corroborates the post-streptococcal trigger; not required to make a presumptive clinical diagnosis or to manage.
Not indicated for a clinically classic mild guttate eruption; reserved for diagnostic uncertainty at specialist review.
There is no anaphylaxis to investigate; ordering an emergency allergy work-up would reflect over-triage of a benign eruption.
